summary: Charles Sherrod was my mentor and the one who gave me my formal education during the Movement. He would come by the SNCC office in the evenings to tutor me, helping me learn to read and understand difficult words. Sherrod handed me books by authors like Jack Kerouac and Richard Wright, and through his guidance, I gained confidence and literacy that I never had in school. I owe him a lot for shaping my education and helping me grow in the Movement.

The Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, or SNCC, was where I spent a lot of time during the Movement. I stayed at their office on Madison Street, especially in the back bedroom, and it was the center of my education and activism. SNCC was the place where I learned to read and grew politically, surrounded by volunteers and leaders like Charles Sherrod. It was truly my school and my home during those years.

Jack Kerouac was one of the authors whose books I read during my time in the Movement. The white kids brought down novels by him and others, and I devoured every one I could find. Reading Kerouac was part of my education, alongside other writers like Richard Wright, and it helped me expand my understanding beyond what I had learned in school. Those books were a big part of how I learned to read and think critically.

Richard Wright was another important author whose works I read while learning to read in the Movement. His books, along with those of Jack Kerouac, were brought down by white volunteers, and I read every one I could get my hands on. Wright's writing helped me understand the struggles and realities of black life, and it was part of the education Charles Sherrod helped me receive. Reading Wright was a key part of my growth during those years.

John Perdew was a white volunteer who performed a one-man play at the center that used to be the Ritz Theater in Harlem. He spoke about how he earned his 'degree' in life itself, or 'Southerology,' by experiencing the South and the Movement firsthand. Perdew's experience was a kind of trade-off education; while he learned about black folks' lives and struggles, I learned to read and gain confidence. He later went back to Harvard with this unique knowledge from the Movement.

The Ritz Theater in Harlem was a place where John Perdew put on his one-man play about his experiences in the South during the Movement. It was a center for sharing stories and performances that reflected the realities of the civil rights struggle. This theater connected the Movement with audiences in Harlem and helped spread awareness through art and education. It was an important cultural spot tied to the activism I was involved in.

Harvard University comes up in my story because John Perdew went back there after his time in the South and the Movement. He had gained what he called a doctorate in 'Southerology,' meaning he had learned firsthand how things worked in the South, especially regarding the Americus Four and their imprisonment. Harvard represents the formal academic world that some volunteers returned to, carrying with them the lessons they learned in the Movement.

The Americus Four were activists who were locked up for sedition during the civil rights struggles in the South. Their case was well known among us in SNCC and the Movement, and it was part of what John Perdew learned about when he was down South. The Americus Four symbolize the harsh repression we faced, but also the courage and sacrifice of those fighting for justice. Their imprisonment was a reminder of the risks involved in our activism.

Stokely Carmichael was a close friend and a strong supporter of mine during the Movement. I remember at a conference in Atlanta when I froze up giving a speech, Stokely jumped down from the balcony, took the microphone, and defended me fiercely against some middle class folks who were mocking me. He called them out boldly and gave me the courage to improve my speaking skills. Later, Stokely married Miriam Makeba and moved to Africa, where he stayed for a long time.

Atlanta was the city where I attended my first big conference at Union Theological Seminary. It was there that I had a tough experience trying to speak to a middle class audience and ended up crying on stage. Stokely Carmichael was there in the balcony and came to my defense, which was a turning point for me. Atlanta represents an important place in my journey of growth and confidence in the Movement.

Union Theological Seminary was the site of a significant conference I attended in Atlanta, where I tried to give a speech but froze up. It was a place where activists and middle class folks gathered, and I learned a hard lesson about public speaking and confidence there. Stokely Carmichael was present and defended me when I was mocked, which helped me resolve to improve myself. The seminary was part of the broader network of institutions connected to the Movement.

Miriam Makeba was the woman Stokely Carmichael married before he moved to Africa. I never saw Stokely again after he went there, as he stayed for a long time. Miriam was part of that connection to Africa and the broader struggle for freedom and justice. Their marriage symbolized a link between the civil rights Movement here and the liberation movements in Africa.

Africa was where Stokely Carmichael went after marrying Miriam Makeba, and he stayed there for a long time. It represented a new chapter in his life and activism, connecting the civil rights struggle in the U.S. to the broader fight against colonialism and oppression on the continent. Africa was a place of significance for many in the Movement who saw the global dimension of our struggle for justice.
